title: Evaluation by electrochemical tests of the passive film stability of equiatomic Ni‐Ti alloy also in presence of stress‐induced martensite
abstract:
Evaluation by electrochemical tests of the passive film
stability of equiatomic Ni-Ti alloy also in presence of
stress-induced martensite
Gianni Rondelli, Bruno Vicentini
CNR-TEMPE, Via Cozzi 53, 20125 Milano, Italy
Received 9 August 1999; accepted 10 November 1999
Abstract:
In this study, potentiodynamic polarization
scans, potentiostatic scratch tests, and modified American
Society for Testing and Materials F746 tests were carried out
in simulated body fluids on commercial orthodontic wires
made of different classes of materials and on titanium used
as a reference. The stability of passivating film, evaluated by
electrochemical techniques that abruptly damage it, e.g., po-
tentiostatic scratch test, increased in the following order:
Ni-Ti # stainless steel < Co-Cr alloy < Ti. Because satisfac-
tory biocompatibilty of implants relies on the presence of a
stable and efficient self-repairing passive film, this ranking
should be considered in view of in vivo applications. More-
over, scratch tests show that it is not possible to enhance the
performance of Ni-Ti samples by modifying surface-passive
film by dipping in an HF/HNO3 mixture. Finally, straining
of Ni-Ti wires under superelastic conditions and consequent
presence of stress-induced martensite does not substantially
modify their localized corrosion resistance. © 2000 John
Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Biomed Mater Res, 51, 47–54, 2000.
Key words: Ni-Ti; passive film; electrochemical test; biocom-
patibility; martensite
INTRODUCTION
Equiatomic Ni-Ti alloy (Ni-50 at % Ti-50 at %) has
been widely exploited as a biomaterial for its shape
memory and superelasticity effect. Regarding its bio-
compatibility, besides titanium, which is generally
considered a well-tolerated biomaterial, this alloy also
contains 56 wt % nickel, whose biocompatibilty has
been questioned inter alia as being a major cause of
contact dermatitis. Vandenkerkhove and coworkers2
reported from the literature data that the biocompat-
ibility is often contradictory; nevertheless, they af-
firmed that no severe damage has been found in living
tissue in direct contact with Ni-Ti. In a paper appear-
ing in the 1970s, Castelman and coworker3 concluded
that the biocompatibilty of Ni-Ti was sufficient to pro-
mote study of possible applications. More recent re-
views4,5 portray a favorable picture of the biocompati-
bilty of Ni-Ti. Shabalovskaya6 and Van Humbeek and
coworkers7 stated that the biocompatility of Ni-Ti is
comparable with that of stainless steels, Co-based al-
loys, and titanium alloys. Ryhanen4 concluded that it
is similar to or better than stainless steel and Ti-6Al-4V.
Some authors8,9 found that, as with pure titanium,
the passivating film present on Ni-Ti is mainly consti-
tuted of TiO2; actually, the good biocompatibilty of
Ni-Ti has been attributed7–9 to the TiO2-based oxides’
passivating film. Accordingly, Oshida and Miyazaki5
assumed that it is pragmatically important to evaluate
the stability of the surface oxide film formed on Ni-Ti
alloy. Nakayama and coworkers10 claimed that mea-
surement of the strength of passivity film is the first
step in evaluating biocompatibility of the alloys.
Because of the foregoing, we deemed it interesting
to perform electrochemical tests to assess the stability
of passivating film on Ni-Ti. The work was conducted
on orthodontic wires representing a type of commer-
cial device easily available; several wires with differ-
ent shapes and dimensions, from different producers,
were examined to see whether it was possible to ob-
tain an overall picture of the behavior of Ni-Ti alloy
independently of the specific factory and proprietary
processing procedures. For comparison purposes,
other materials widely used as implants, such as stain-
less steel, Co-Cr alloy, and titanium, also were consid-
ered.
Another purpose of this work was to verify the in-
fluence of stress-induced martensite on the localized
corrosion behavior of Ni-Ti, as this can form in service,
e.g., when exploiting the superelastic properties.
Correspondence to: G. Rondelli
© 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
 MATERIALS AND METHODS
Materials
Table I reports the materials studied. The wires were ex-
amined in the as-received condition; only Wire e was sub-
jected to dipping in HF/HNO3
8 and further passivating in
air for at least 12 h, since in potentiodynamic tests per-
formed in the as-received condition, it exhibited insufficient
corrosion performance.11
Figure 1 shows stress–strain curves for the Ni-Ti wires
examined; the curves were determined at 40°C with a 1455
Zwick tensile machine equipped with a 2KN loading cell,
under strain control at strain rate of 0.6%/min. The curve for
Wire c, not reported in the figure for clarity, was similar to
Wire b. The transformation temperatures were evaluated by
measuring the variation of resistance versus temperature,
using an experimental arrangement consisting of an ESI
1701 b digital ohmmeter and a microcryostat equipped with
a temperature controller.
Aggressive environments
All tests were conducted at 40 ± 1°C.The following solu-
tions were used: (a) artificial saliva12: KCl 1.47 g/L, NaHCO3
1.25 g/L, KSCN 0.52 g/L, KH2PO4 ? H2O 0.19 g/L; lactic
acid added to attain pH 6.7; (b) 0.9% NaCl.
Electrochemical tests apparatus
The cell shown in Figure 2 was specifically designed and
realized to perform electrochemical tests on wires. The hy-
draulic seal at the bottom of the cell was achieved using a
silicon plug suitably pierced so that the wire to be tested
could be fitted exactly into the hole; the plug was then
pressed into the conic socket at the bottom of the cell. Using
this simple arrangement, crevice attack in correspondence
with the contact area between the wire and the silicone plug
did not occur. Figure 2 also shows the test rack that allowed
the application of a prefixed strain to the wires: By rotating
the ring nut, an elongation of 1 mm/turn was obtained (0.6%
strain/turn). A tungsten carbide conical stylus (radius, 50
mm) fixed at the end of an arm operating through a spherical
joint allowed scratching of wires.
Potentiodynamic tests
After the cell was placed in the test solution, it was purged
for 1 h using high-purity nitrogen (about 20 L/h flow rate);
purging was continued at a reduced speed throughout the
test duration. The potentiodynamic measurements were per-
formed according to the G 61 and G 5 Standard Test Meth-
ods13–15 at a scanning rate of 600 mV h−1 and starting from

bilty of Ni-Ti. Shabalovskaya6 and Van Humbeek and
coworkers7 stated that the biocompatility of Ni-Ti is

alloy. Nakayama and coworkers10 claimed that mea-
surement of the strength of passivity film is the first

cial device easily available; several wires with differ-

−800 mV versus SCE. The potential corresponding to an

Pitting potential determined by potentiodynamic tests
may be more noble with respect to the static pitting poten-
tial, because pitting initiation can be a very slow process.15
Pessal and Liu16 proposed a method of measuring pitting
potential that does not depend on the pit initiation time. In
this method, the passive surface is scratched at various po-
tentials. The test is carried out starting from a potential value
lower than the pitting potential measured by the potentio-
dynamic method; this is maintained for a period of 2 min, at
the end of which scratching is performed. The scratch pro-
duced a sharp increase in anodic current followed by either
rapid lowering to the starting value for potential values
lower than the pitting potential, or by a subsequent continu-
ous increase for potential values higher than the pitting po-
tential. In the first case, the current was monitored for 10
min; the applied potential was then increased by 20 mV and
was held unchanged for 2 min, after which scratching was
performed again. In the second case, the pits were left to
grow until a current value of about 0.3 mA was reached.
Figure 3 shows the profile of current versus time during a
Modified American Society for Testing and
The ASTM F746 Standard Test Method17 was set up by
ASTM as a screening test to rank surgical implant alloys
with respect to their resistance to localized (pitting and crev-
ice) corrosion in 0.9% NaCl solution.
In the ASTM F746 test, an electrochemical damage of the
passive film is achieved by abruptly applying a potential
impulse of +800mV versus SCE during the stimulation step.
Figure 4 shows various cases that can happen during this
step: If after 15 min localized corrosion has not occurred
(Case E) the test is terminated and the material is considered
immune to localized corrosion; otherwise, a voltage step
back to a preselected potential is made (repassivation step).
The test consists of alternating steps between stimulation at
+800 mV and repassivation to a preselected potential, up to
a critical potential at which repassivation does not take
place. The increase in potential value between each repassi-
Although the original test procedure established that the
crevice conditions must be produced by fitting PTFE tapered
collars on cylindrical specimens, in the present study the
crevice conditions were achieved by means of two acetal
resin discs tightened on the wire with a 0.2 N ? m torque.11
Our tests were conducted at 40°C instead of 37°C.
Figure 5 shows examples of potentiodynamic
curves obtained in 0.9% NaCl. Wire e in the as-
Testing rack for conducting electrochemical tests
Current-time plot for Ni-Ti wire “d” during
scratch test in 0.9% NaCl solution at 40°C.
ELECTROCHEMICAL TESTS ON Ni-Ti
received condition did not present a passive zone,
whereas after passivation its pitting resistance in-
creased noticeably (no pitting up to 800 mV vs. SCE).
The Co-Cr alloy did not present attack up to high
potential values and the increase of current that took
place around 600 mV versus SCE was due to dissolu-
tion of hexavalent chromium. In this environment, ti-
tanium was immune from pitting attack.
In Figures 6 and 7 and 10–12, the maximum poten-
tial value considered was fixed to +800mV versus
SCE, for two reasons. On the one hand, if one wants to
compare the breakdown potentials from different
tests, it must be considered that with the ASTM F746
method the potential of +800mV versus SCE as thresh-
old for immunity to localized corrosion is established;
second, the values of potential that implant materials
attain in vivo are in the range of 100–500 mV versus
SCE18; hence, higher potential test values are not
Figure 6 gives the influence of strain on potentiody-
namic pitting potentials. No effect of 4% strain, and
hence of the presence of stress-induced martensite,
was observed. In a previous study,11 a different lot of
Ni-Ti Wire Type a gave a pitting potential of about 400
mV versus SCE, whereas in this study no pitting was
observed, which is in accordance with the results of
Strietzel and Gebhardt,19 who found differences in the
corrosion behavior of orthodontic wires from different
lots. For the Co-Cr alloy, the comments made regard-
ing Figure 5 should be remembered.
Figure 7 shows the influence of strain on localized
corrosion potentials evaluated by the modified ASTM
F746 method. In this case, no effect of 4% strain on the
localized corrosion behavior of Ni-Ti alloy was de-
Typical potentiodynamic polarization curves in
deaerated 0.9% NaCl solution at 40°C for orthodontic wires;
Schematic plot of current-time during the stimu-
lation step of ASTM F746 test (in cases A, B and D stimula-
tion occurred; in case E the material is immune to localized
Pitting potentials of the orthodontic wires ob-
tained by potentiodynamic scans in deaerated 0.9% NaCl at
40°C in presence or not of 4% strain (average of at least three
samples and standard deviation; for identification of wires
from “a” to “h”, see Table 1).
Regarding Wires a and e, during the first run of the
stimulation step, the current density almost instanta-
neously reached the critical value of 500 mAcm−2 (the
behavior of Type A in Fig. 4). In the following stimu-
lation treatment, after the repassivation step, we ob-
served that the time to reach the critical current den-
sity value increased to demonstrate the behavior of
Type B. By further repeating stimulation cycles, the
behavior of Type D and eventually of Type E was
observed. On these materials, repetition of stimulation
and repassivation cycles produced chemical surface
modifications that eventually stabilized the surface
film. On the basis of ASTM F746 specification, Wires a
and e should be considered immune from localized
corrosion. However, the relatively high anodic current
values circulating during the stimulation steps had
some effects; in fact, by observing the specimen under
the scanning electron microscope, localized attacks
On Co-Cr alloy, current densities exceeding the
critical value of 5 mAcm−2 at repassivation potentials
$ 600 mV versus SCE were obtained. By strictly ap-
plying the ASTM F746 specification, the material
should be considered susceptible to localized corro-
sion; however, as previously remarked (see comments
on Fig. 5), these current densities are not associated
with the onset of localized corrosion; therefore, we
considered this material immune to localized corro-
Figure 9 plots the pitting potentials on Wire b ob-
tained by the scratch technique in 0.9% NaCl at 40°C
as a function of strain levels. No effect of strain was
detectable; by extrapolating the pitting potential at
zero strain, it can be argued that the value so obtained
is representative of unstrained wires. For our scratch
assembly to work, the wire had to be slightly stressed.
Figure 10 reports the pitting potential values ob-
tained by potentiostatic scratch tests in 0.9% NaCl and
artificial saliva. Ni-Ti wires had pitting potentials be-

tween 100 and 200 mV; passivated Wire e has behav-
ior similar to the other Ni-Ti samples. For Ni-Ti wires,
low pitting potential values were obtained in both
0.9% NaCl and the more diluted artificial saliva solu-
tion. The stainless-steel 18Cr-8Ni behaved slightly bet-

reconfirmation of the raking of pitting corrosion resis-

heats.21 Regarding the Co-Cr alloy, at potentials above
600 mV versus SCE, an increase of current indepen-
dent of the scratch occurs, but since no sign of attack
was observed on specimens, the material was consid-
ered immune to pitting (see comments on Fig. 5).
Figure 11 shows the relationship between the pit-

the potentiodynamic tests and those by potentiostatic

obtained at 40°C in 0.9% NaCl using the three differ-
ent test methods: potentiodynamic scans, modified
ASTM F746 tests, and potentiostatic scratch tests.
Apart from the anomalous results of Ni-Ti Wires a and
e in the ASTM F746 tests (see discussion on Fig. 7), the
techniques in which the passive film was abruptly per-
turbed (i.e., potentiostatic scratch and ASTM F746
tests) gave breakdown corrosion potentials that were
similar and more conservative than tests in which the
electrode potential was increased slowly (i.e., the po-

standard deviation; for identification of wires from “a” to
As mentioned in the Introduction, one possible ap-
proach to the assessment of biocompatibility of alloys
is through evaluation of the stability of the passivating
film. In effect, it can be supposed that satisfactory bio-
compatibility can be obtained only in presence of a
passive film that is stable, allows very low ion release,
and is capable of self-reconstruction if locally dam-
aged. In this connection, the potentiodynamic tests
show for the Ni-Ti alloy a corrosion behavior gener-
ally comparable, from a practical point of view, to the
18Cr-8Ni stainless steel, and in cases of optimized fi-
nal surface treatments, to Co-Cr alloy and titanium.
However, tests in which abrupt damage of the passive
film takes place (i.e., the scratch test) indicated a low
repassivation capacity for the Ni-Ti alloy, which in
this case should be considered comparable to stainless
steel independent of any passivating surface treat-
ments. The poor self-repairing tendency of the passive
film, once damaged, could represent a weak point for
Surface treatment (i.e., dipping in acid mixtures fol-
lowed by passivation in air), which led to the forma-
tion of amorphous-like21 or more uniform surface ox-
ide film,22 evidenced by potentiodynamic tests a great
enhancement in the localized corrosion resistance;
however, such treatments did not modify the intrinsic
proneness of the alloy bulk to develop a protective
film in operative conditions, as evidenced by the low
values of the breakdown potential determined by the
potentiostatic scratch technique. Therefore, in view of
practical applications, Cr-Co alloy and titanium offer a
Finally, our tests indicate no appreciable influence
of stress-induced martensite on localized corrosion re-
sistance of Ni-Ti alloy; i.e., the corrosion performances
does not depend on the crystallographic structure, but
on the chemical composition of the alloy. This is in
accordance with the findings of Sinigaglia and co-
workers23 and Baghdasarian and coworker,24 who,
dealing with stainless steels of the series AISI 300 and
TRIP steels, respectively, found the localized corrosion
resistance to be independent of the presence of mar-
On the basis of in vitro electrochemical tests con-
ducted in simulated body fluids on orthodontic wires,
the following conclusions can be drawn.
1. Potentiodynamic tests, which imply slow poten-
tial scans without mechanical damage to the sur-
face film, showed for the Ni-Ti a pitting corrosion
resistance similar to that with stainless steel and
specimens passivated by dipping in a suitable
HF/HNO3 mixture, comparable even to Co-Cr
2. In tests that allow evaluation the self-repairing
capacity of passivating film, once abruptly dam-
aged, i.e., performed by the scratch technique,
localized corrosion resistance increases in the fol-
lowing order: Ni-Ti # stainless steel < Co-Cr al-
loy < Ti. Hence, taking into account the key role
played by passive film in biocompatibilty, Ti and
Co-Cr seem to offer a higher margin of safety for
3. The presence of stress-induced martensite does
not appreciably modify the localized corrosion
The authors thank T. Ranucci for technical assistance in
physical characterization of Ni-Ti wires, and A. Bianchessi
and G. Turisini for help in the project and realization of the
electrochemical test apparatus.
